channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - www.mmorpg.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for www.mmorpg.com

item.title: Sea of Thieves Begins New Bilge Rats Weekender Event, Resets Doubloons and Gives Opportunities to Earn Some
item.content:encoded:
Sea of Thieves has begun its first new Bilge Rats Weekender event, reset Doubloons, and more in the latest update.

There are lots of changes in the update, including new items , everything at the Outpost is now available for gold, and more related to the Doubloon reset. This decision was done to help the SoT economy. You can still earn them, but with fewer opportunities to do so (like Seasonal Renown), and there’s a wallet cap of 4,000 to prevent hoarding, which was one reason for the reset in the first place. One of the best new ways to earn yourself some Doubloons is in the Bilge Rats Weekender event.

The event is longer this time for its debut, running from today through April 20th, and it’s pretty packed. You can get full details in the patch notes, but step into the game and see decorated outposts and seaposts, and head to the tavern to find Larinna and learn about everything on offer (and for access to the Bilge Rats Black Market).

There are two event exclusive Bilge Rats Voyages, “The Smuggler’s Doubloon Run” and “Recovery of a Secret Shipment”, both of which will reward Doubloons and more. Bilge Rats Challenges are also available, with some lighter, more playful activities to complete solo or in a crew. These will also reward some Doubloons.

The Festival of Fishing is back and, going forward, each Bilge Rats Weekender event will feature the return of a popular past live event. There are two limited Voyages in High Seas, event goals to complete, and yes, more Doubloons. The Hunter’s Call offers some useful bonuses too.

At 9:00 p.m. game time, one Outpost will host a fireworks display, and let everyone know the best viewing locations. info will be available in advance if you want to stake out a spot. You can also buy a fireworks crate to take  part. 

The Bilge Rats Black Market and Outpost shops have also restocked, now with everything for gold.



https://www.mmorpg.com/news/sea-of-thieves-begins-new-bilge-rats-weekender-event-resets-doubloons-and-gives-opportunities-to-earn-some-2000137818
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Ashes of Creation Founder Posts Court Case Filings, Including Evidence About Alleged Loan-To-Buy Scheme, Intimidation, And More
item.content:encoded:
In the midst of intense accusations about misappropriation of funds, Ashes of Creation and Intrepid Studios lead Steven Sharif has put out a new statement that unwraps a slew of court-submitted evidence alleging misconduct from Intrepid’s former board. 

The latest release from Sharif includes a 12-page “statement,” which then leaks out to a copy of a Memo regarding Sharif’s Motion for Receiver, the filed witness declarations, and all of the filed exhibits which include loan filings, texts, and emails (and the latter is 195 pages, so click with caution). 

In short, the legal documents show an end-of-life legal and financial spiral for the company, plus conflicts over Sharif’s miscommunications over several years, as well as Board members’ maneuvering around these situations as well as personnel mismanagement. The following goes over some of the more substantial tidbits found in the presented evidence. 

It should be noted that we need to maintain that these are allegations, and Sharif’s positioning here is obviously intended as a defense in both a legal sense and the court of public opinion. Like with Caramanis’ claims over the last few months, it should be taken with a grain of salt, as we won’t know the full truth until this plays out in court.

An end-of-life legal spiral

The long and short of it is that Sharif is accusing the Board of a “loan to own” scheme, directing the company towards bankruptcy to further invest and, in turn, claim ownership amid corporate distress. In Sharif’s own words: 

"As alleged in our federal complaint, and further supported by the sworn declarations and  documentary evidence now filed with the Court, the Board defendants, led by Chairman Rob Dawson, engineered a plan to seize control of Intrepid’s assets for their own benefit through threats, coercion, deception, and unlawful self-dealing with zero regard for the  company they destroyed and the human lives damaged by their illegal conduct,” the statement reads. “That plan involved starving a viable company of cash to manufacture a default, using fear and leverage to force signatures and concessions, terminating more than 200 people while stripping them of wages, benefits, and accrued obligations, leaving vendors and creditors high and dry, and then using the crisis they created to justify a foreclosure designed to transfer Intrepid’s assets and intellectual property into an entity controlled by Robert Dawson. "

He also accuses Dawson of trying to defraud a bank while redirecting Steam profits into his own company, the TFE Holdings, one that had previously come up in court files. 

Despite the reputation that Ashes of Creation had over the years, just before the time of the shutdown, Steam sales weren’t so bad for its reputation thus far. There were 199,493 total purchases on the Steam platform, totaling $8.2 million for Intrepid Studios. With Steam Key usage added, there were over 276,000 players total via Steam. 

In the evidence, texts show Dawson pushing for the Steam transactions to go through before foreclosure proceedings, before the assets and ownership of the company as a whole are transferred to TFE Holdings.  

The Board that stayed out of sight 

In the aftermath of the initial shutdown, investor Jason Caramanis claimed that Sharif himself wanted to keep the Board on the down-low because “multi-level marketing is stinky, and people in the gaming community think they're all pyramids and stinky.” However, it’s still worth noting that Caramanis himself is named in a settled lawsuit regarding a multi-level marketing company. 

Beyond that, through the evidence now presented, it does seem like the Board behind Intrepid was proactively avoiding being seen—including by the primary subject of concern from the Board, Rob Dawson. 

“I want you guys in the lead and me hidden in the background as ownership,” one text from Dawson reads. “Never would be in the forefront. I want to get the legal and finance buttoned up so we are good to go”.

He also claims, in the wake of the Riot discussions, that he would always vote with Sharif, though he takes a somewhat passive-aggressive approach to discouraging the deal (all of which will be highlighted later).  

However, Sharif claims that Dawson attempted “strong-arm tactics” to “expand his control,” including visiting Caramanis, who Sharif claims is “a violent individual with multiple prior arrests,” including one arrest around 2009. (MMORPG cannot find any record of Caramanis’s arrest(s) via online search.) Sharif in the witness declarations claims Caramanis would “yell at and threaten me in [Dawson’s] stead, acting as an ‘enforcer’” to further Dawson’s agenda. 

Also in the evidence are several very forward texts from both Dawson and Caramanis, ranging from stalling action to outright aggressive language. Some texts from Dawson threatened to hold wire payments, explicitly holding Intrepid’s payroll over Sharif’s head. Dawson stated he would come after Sharif’s business partner and husband John Moore “with a vengeance of he doesn’t sign. He fucjed [sic] my life and i have severe animosity towards him”. 

Caramanis more often took the aggressive angle, calling Sharif a “MOTher fucker” and a “bitch,” stating “i am talking to you like you need to understand who your [sic] fucking with.” 

In the more alarming set of texts, he claims that papers needed to be “fucken done tomorrow or your life is over On Tuesday when business opens i am NOT fucking around with you asshole anymore”. He also claimed that if he didn’t have Dawson’s back, “i would fuck your whole world up [...] hope i am clear your a piece of shit.” 

Employee restructuring concerns 

Payroll concerns came to a head towards the last few months of Intrepid’s life. Emails are shown from Sharif attempting to coordinate with the Board to continue health insurance for employees through the insurance companies Cigna and Kaiser. In the legal documents, Sharif claims these sorts of disruptions happened due to Dawson attempting to extort more ownership of the company. 

While it’s unclear if Sharif agreed to layoffs at all, it appears the lack of post-layoff severance benefits for the first wave of employees weren’t of Sharif’s design, but of Board member Aaron Bartels, who claimed that the employees weren’t valuable enough to warrant a severance package, nor legally-entitled PTO, and that the closure of the company would do that work for them. 

“I do not believe we should pay severance nor PTO,” an email from Bartels reads. These are bottom performers. Who have already been paid more than they deserve. We don’t owe them more. 

“Any suit against us will be against the old entity that will have no value in a week. So unless Sam thinks we have an obligation or exposure. Let’s not pay either.” The suit may reference the WARN Act, which, indeed, may be filed for layoffs of a certain size, and was later invoked after the mass studio closure. 

Then, after Sharif resigned, the Board took action to move the Director of Operations, and Sharif’s husband, Rob Moore into a CFO role—without his permission, or even awareness of the situation. This is not only a personnel conundrum, but also a legal one, as California has strict requirements about registering C-suite executives. 

Moore resigned directly after that in part due to this, but also because he was aware of the foreclosure scenario as pointed out by Sharif, and because he claims the Board was directly harassing his wife throughout. 

Riot discussions that fell through 

In prior discussions, Caramanis stated that there was possible Riot Games involvement, and now we know this to be true via an email presented as part of the evidence. A December 2022 email exchange between Intrepid, Via Steven and John Moore, and Riot via CFO Mark Rottosanti, Marc Merrill, Nicolo Laurent and others shows initial discussions between the two corporations about the benefits of some form of investment, which would be between $250 and 500 million. 

Since then, Merrill has clarified that Riot considered “making an investment or even acquiring Intrepid a few years ago, but Riot decided not to move forward with discussions.” 

“The team there shared our goal of making the next great MMO — something we’re more committed to than ever — but combining efforts ultimately didn’t make sense for us,” the post on Twitter/X reads. 

Regarding the Ashes of Creation / Riot rumors (shoutout /r/mmorpg), we looked at making an investment or even acquiring Intrepid a few years ago, but Riot decided not to move forward with discussions.The team there shared our goal of making the next great MMO — something we’re…

— Tryndamere (@MarcMerrill) April 15, 2026

Caramanis’s past explanation of this situation implied that Sharif and Intrepid were actually leaning towards this possible investment. However, Sharif claims that Dawson “dissuaded” him from taking the offer, with discussions seeming to drag out with Dawson into 2023. 

“I’m not excited and all about that when I do the math on it, but I want to hear your thoughts,” a transcript of a voice mail that February reads. Then, in July, he takes a more pointed stance on it after a Board meeting or vote, the details of which are withheld, seems to go awry. 

“Remember you can’t sell the Corp assets without shareholder consent”, and “This is going to end up ugly but if u think better to have riot vote and control than me, then by all means go for if” [sic]. 

Again, this is all presented keeping in mind that this is only evidence selected by Sharif and his legal team. Notably, there’s nothing for now to proactively contradict any claims of misuse of funds, save for a few compliments on Sarif’s progress with Ashes of Creation—we would have to await any evidence in any lawsuits accusing him of misconduct. 

For ease of catching up, here are other important moments to note in this legal saga: 

  • Ashes of Creation's Steven Sharif Resigned 'In Protest' As Intrepids Board Starts Mass Layoffs At MMO Studio (Plus Update) (January 31)
  • Ashes of Creation's Former Comms Director Releases Statement, Giving Brief Timeline Of Intrepid's Shut Down (February 3)
  • Labor Lawsuits Filed Against Ashes of Creation Studio Intrepid Games, Pointing To WARN Act (February 7)
  • Steven Sharif Sues Intrepid's Board, Detailing His Side of What Happened With AoC and Alleging Smear Campaign Against Him (February 18) 
  • Ashes of Creation IP's New Owner Gets Restraining Order Compelling Return of Company Assets & Account Info from Steven Sharif (February 20)
  • Sharif's Injunction Request Denied, IP Sale Reversed, But Company Still Wants to Sell (April 3)
  • Alleged Ashes of Creation Financial Ledger Leaked Claiming Misappropriation Of Studio Funds; Sharif Responds (April 13) 

And as a reminder that there are real people dealing with the fallout: 

  • Former Intrepid Employees Weigh in on Ashes of Creation, Development, Management, and How They're Doing Now 

Victoria Rose

Victoria has professionally written about games since 2016, including small former tenures with Polygon and Fanbyte. She mostly spends time in FFXIV, head-deep in roleplay campaigns or stubbornly playing Black Mage through high-end raids. Former obsessions include Dota 2 and The Secret World (also mostly roleplaying). Come visit their estate: Diabolos (Crystal DC), Goblet, Ward 4, Plot 28.



https://www.mmorpg.com/news/ashes-of-creation-founder-posts-court-case-filings-including-evidence-about-alleged-loan-to-buy-scheme-intimidation-and-more-2000137821
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Ashes of Creation Founder Posts Court Case Filings, Including Evidence About Alleged Loan-To-Buy Scheme, Physical Threats, And More
item.content:encoded:
In the midst of intense accusations about misappropriation of funds, Ashes of Creation and Intrepid Studios lead Steven Sharif has put out a new statement that unwraps a slew of court-submitted evidence alleging misconduct from Intrepid’s former board. 

The latest release from Sharif includes a 12-page “statement,” which then leaks out to a copy of a Memo regarding Sharif’s Motion for Receiver, the filed witness declarations, and all of the filed exhibits which include loan filings, texts, and emails (and the latter is 195 pages, so click with caution). 

In short, the legal documents show an end-of-life legal and financial spiral for the company, plus conflicts over Sharif’s miscommunications over several years, as well as Board members’ maneuvering around these situations as well as personnel mismanagement. The following goes over some of the more substantial tidbits found in the presented evidence. 

It should be noted that we need to maintain that these are allegations, and Sharif’s positioning here is obviously intended as a defense in both a legal sense and the court of public opinion. Like with Caramanis’ claims over the last few months, it should be taken with a grain of salt, as we won’t know the full truth until this plays out in court.

An end-of-life legal spiral

The long and short of it is that Sharif is accusing the Board of a “loan to own” scheme, directing the company towards bankruptcy to further invest and, in turn, claim ownership amid corporate distress. In Sharif’s own words: 

"As alleged in our federal complaint, and further supported by the sworn declarations and  documentary evidence now filed with the Court, the Board defendants, led by Chairman Rob Dawson, engineered a plan to seize control of Intrepid’s assets for their own benefit through threats, coercion, deception, and unlawful self-dealing with zero regard for the  company they destroyed and the human lives damaged by their illegal conduct,” the statement reads. “That plan involved starving a viable company of cash to manufacture a default, using fear and leverage to force signatures and concessions, terminating more than 200 people while stripping them of wages, benefits, and accrued obligations, leaving vendors and creditors high and dry, and then using the crisis they created to justify a foreclosure designed to transfer Intrepid’s assets and intellectual property into an entity controlled by Robert Dawson. "

He also accuses Dawson of trying to defraud a bank while redirecting Steam profits into his own company, the TFE Holdings, one that had previously come up in court files. 

Despite the reputation that Ashes of Creation had over the years, just before the time of the shutdown, Steam sales weren’t so bad for its reputation thus far. There were 199,493 total purchases on the Steam platform, totaling $8.2 million for Intrepid Studios. With Steam Key usage added, there were over 276,000 players total via Steam. 

In the evidence, texts show Dawson pushing for the Steam transactions to go through before foreclosure proceedings, before the assets and ownership of the company as a whole are transferred to TFE Holdings.  

The Board that stayed out of sight 

In the aftermath of the initial shutdown, investor Jason Caramanis claimed that Sharif himself wanted to keep the Board on the down-low because “multi-level marketing is stinky, and people in the gaming community think they're all pyramids and stinky.” However, it’s still worth noting that Caramanis himself is named in a settled lawsuit regarding a multi-level marketing company. 

Beyond that, through the evidence now presented, it does seem like the Board behind Intrepid was proactively avoiding being seen—including by the primary subject of concern from the Board, Rob Dawson. 

“I want you guys in the lead and me hidden in the background as ownership,” one text from Dawson reads. “Never would be in the forefront. I want to get the legal and finance buttoned up so we are good to go”.

He also claims, in the wake of the Riot discussions, that he would always vote with Sharif, though he takes a somewhat passive-aggressive approach to discouraging the deal (all of which will be highlighted later).  

However, Sharif claims that Dawson attempted “strong-arm tactics” to “expand his control,” including visiting Caramanis, who Sharif claims is “a violent individual with multiple prior arrests,” including one arrest around 2009. (MMORPG cannot find any record of Caramanis’s arrest(s) via online search.) Sharif in the witness declarations claims Caramanis would “yell at and threaten me in [Dawson’s] stead, acting as an ‘enforcer’” to further Dawson’s agenda. 

Also in the evidence are several very forward texts from both Dawson and Caramanis, ranging from stalling action to outright aggressive language. Some texts from Dawson threatened to hold wire payments, explicitly holding Intrepid’s payroll over Sharif’s head. Dawson stated he would come after Sharif’s business partner and husband John Moore “with a vengeance of he doesn’t sign. He fucjed [sic] my life and i have severe animosity towards him”. 

Caramanis more often took the aggressive angle, calling Sharif a “MOTher fucker” and a “bitch,” stating “i am talking to you like you need to understand who your [sic] fucking with.” 

In the more alarming set of texts, he claims that papers needed to be “fucken done tomorrow or your life is over On Tuesday when business opens i am NOT fucking around with you asshole anymore”. He also claimed that if he didn’t have Dawson’s back, “i would fuck your whole world up [...] hope i am clear your a piece of shit.” 

Employee restructuring concerns 

Payroll concerns came to a head towards the last few months of Intrepid’s life. Emails are shown from Sharif attempting to coordinate with the Board to continue health insurance for employees through the insurance companies Cigna and Kaiser. In the legal documents, Sharif claims these sorts of disruptions happened due to Dawson attempting to extort more ownership of the company. 

While it’s unclear if Sharif agreed to layoffs at all, it appears the lack of post-layoff severance benefits for the first wave of employees weren’t of Sharif’s design, but of Board member Aaron Bartels, who claimed that the employees weren’t valuable enough to warrant a severance package, nor legally-entitled PTO, and that the closure of the company would do that work for them. 

“I do not believe we should pay severance nor PTO,” an email from Bartels reads. These are bottom performers. Who have already been paid more than they deserve. We don’t owe them more. 

“Any suit against us will be against the old entity that will have no value in a week. So unless Sam thinks we have an obligation or exposure. Let’s not pay either.” The suit may reference the WARN Act, which, indeed, may be filed for layoffs of a certain size, and was later invoked after the mass studio closure. 

Then, after Sharif resigned, the Board took action to move the Director of Operations, and Sharif’s husband, Rob Moore into a CFO role—without his permission, or even awareness of the situation. This is not only a personnel conundrum, but also a legal one, as California has strict requirements about registering C-suite executives. 

Moore resigned directly after that in part due to this, but also because he was aware of the foreclosure scenario as pointed out by Sharif, and because he claims the Board was directly harassing his wife throughout. 

Riot discussions that fell through 

In prior discussions, Caramanis stated that there was possible Riot Games involvement, and now we know this to be true via an email presented as part of the evidence. A December 2022 email exchange between Intrepid, Via Steven and John Moore, and Riot via CFO Mark Rottosanti, Marc Merrill, Nicolo Laurent and others shows initial discussions between the two corporations about the benefits of some form of investment, which would be between $250 and 500 million. 

Since then, Merrill has clarified that Riot considered “making an investment or even acquiring Intrepid a few years ago, but Riot decided not to move forward with discussions.” 

“The team there shared our goal of making the next great MMO — something we’re more committed to than ever — but combining efforts ultimately didn’t make sense for us,” the post on Twitter/X reads. 

Regarding the Ashes of Creation / Riot rumors (shoutout /r/mmorpg), we looked at making an investment or even acquiring Intrepid a few years ago, but Riot decided not to move forward with discussions.The team there shared our goal of making the next great MMO — something we’re…

— Tryndamere (@MarcMerrill) April 15, 2026

Caramanis’s past explanation of this situation implied that Sharif and Intrepid were actually leaning towards this possible investment. However, Sharif claims that Dawson “dissuaded” him from taking the offer, with discussions seeming to drag out with Dawson into 2023. 

“I’m not excited and all about that when I do the math on it, but I want to hear your thoughts,” a transcript of a voice mail that February reads. Then, in July, he takes a more pointed stance on it after a Board meeting or vote, the details of which are withheld, seems to go awry. 

“Remember you can’t sell the Corp assets without shareholder consent”, and “This is going to end up ugly but if u think better to have riot vote and control than me, then by all means go for if” [sic]. 

Again, this is all presented keeping in mind that this is only evidence selected by Sharif and his legal team. Notably, there’s nothing for now to proactively contradict any claims of misuse of funds, save for a few compliments on Sarif’s progress with Ashes of Creation—we would have to await any evidence in any lawsuits accusing him of misconduct. 

For ease of catching up, here are other important moments to note in this legal saga: 

  • Ashes of Creation's Steven Sharif Resigned 'In Protest' As Intrepids Board Starts Mass Layoffs At MMO Studio (Plus Update) (January 31)
  • Ashes of Creation's Former Comms Director Releases Statement, Giving Brief Timeline Of Intrepid's Shut Down (February 3)
  • Labor Lawsuits Filed Against Ashes of Creation Studio Intrepid Games, Pointing To WARN Act (February 7)
  • Steven Sharif Sues Intrepid's Board, Detailing His Side of What Happened With AoC and Alleging Smear Campaign Against Him (February 18) 
  • Ashes of Creation IP's New Owner Gets Restraining Order Compelling Return of Company Assets & Account Info from Steven Sharif (February 20)
  • Sharif's Injunction Request Denied, IP Sale Reversed, But Company Still Wants to Sell (April 3)
  • Alleged Ashes of Creation Financial Ledger Leaked Claiming Misappropriation Of Studio Funds; Sharif Responds (April 13) 

And as a reminder that there are real people dealing with the fallout: 

  • Former Intrepid Employees Weigh in on Ashes of Creation, Development, Management, and How They're Doing Now 

Victoria Rose

Victoria has professionally written about games since 2016, including small former tenures with Polygon and Fanbyte. She mostly spends time in FFXIV, head-deep in roleplay campaigns or stubbornly playing Black Mage through high-end raids. Former obsessions include Dota 2 and The Secret World (also mostly roleplaying). Come visit their estate: Diabolos (Crystal DC), Goblet, Ward 4, Plot 28.
